title: ipv6_addr.c: snprintf writes 39 constant-width hex chars but still checks truncation
description:
Location: ipv6_addr.c:159-185

lichen_ipv6_addr_to_str() uses snprintf to format an IPv6 address. The output is exactly 39 chars (8 groups * 4 hex + 7 colons). The function already checks buflen >= LICHEN_IPV6_ADDR_STR_LEN (40) at line 152, so the truncation check at line 181-185 can never trigger.

What goes wrong: Defensive coding is fine, but unreachable code paths add cognitive load. A reader wonders 'is there a case where this triggers?' and must trace the math to conclude 'no'.

Suggested fix: Remove the truncation check and add an assertion or BUILD_ASSERT that LICHEN_IPV6_ADDR_STR_LEN >= 40. The early-return at line 152-157 already guarantees sufficient space.

Alternatively: keep the check but add a comment /* cannot happen: output is fixed 39 chars */ to signal intent.
close_reason: Fixed: removed unreachable truncation check, added BUILD_ASSERT to verify buffer size at compile time, updated header doc
